# **Bayesian Inference for Agentic Evaluation of Task Difficulty**

## **1 Introduction, Settings, & Motivation**

The core objective of this project is to derive calibrated estimates of **task difficulty** ($$\\beta$$) for **long-horizon** **agents** based on **human-interpretable** features of the tasks and agents, in two settings:

1. **The Solvable Regime:** For tasks that are solvable by current models, running full evaluations is computationally expensive. Can we accurately predict difficulty in a *cheaper* way than running the agent evaluation itself? In particular, we will use only the problem statement and environment.   
   1. Motivation: these cheap difficulty predictions can be used to sample problems of a useful difficulty during RL, avoiding wasted rollouts on too hard or too easy problems.   
2. **The Frontier Regime:** For tasks beyond current frontier model capabilities, we currently lack signal about precisely how hard they are. Can we estimate *how far* these tasks are from being solved, or understand *what* about agents’ interactions with these tasks makes them so difficult?   
   1.  Motivation 1: We can use these estimates to **forecast** when in the future frontier models will be able to solve specific difficult tasks within the dataset with 50% probability. See [Experiment D](#3.4-experiment-d:-time-horizon-prediction-—-completed) for more details.   
   2. Motivation 2:  Understanding how failure modes relate to difficulty of problems is useful for improving agents. 

In answering these questions, we hope to improve our understanding of what makes a problem difficult, and what signals we get about this from an agent’s failing attempt at solving the problem. It would be especially useful if these signals generalized across datasets. 

---

## **2 Theoretical Framework: Amortized Bayesian Inference**

We will use the IRT framework that every task $$i$$ has a latent difficulty $$\\beta\_i$$. Our main goal throughout will be to provide good estimates of $$\\beta\_i$$ with various affordances. We use the standard IRT model of success probability of an agent $$j$$ on task $$i$$: 

$$P(y\_{ij} \= 1 \\mid \\theta\_j, \\alpha\_i, \\beta\_i) \= \\sigma(\\alpha\_i(\\theta\_j \- \\beta\_i))$$

To address the solvable regime setting, we will train one linear model that predicts a prior estimate of this latent difficulty just using textual data and metadata of the problem and access to the problem environment from Lunette. [Truong et al. (2025)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.13335) basically did this but for Q\&A benchmarks and without environment access (since no such thing exists).  

To address the frontier regime setting for when we observe a new problem \+ failing trajectory data, we train a linear model to predict the update to our prior difficulty estimates. 

### 2.2 Goal 1: The No Trajectory Prior

**Objective:** Given a held out set of tasks, predict the probability of agents succeeding or failing on those tasks. 

For our difficulty predictor model: 

* **Input ($$x\_i$$):** For each task, we will get a vector of features of the task using LLM as judge on the task. For her project, Daria will be using embeddings of the problem text \+ metadata for this $$x\_i$$.   
  * I would like to use some kind of automated evolutionary method for getting better LLM as judge prompts. Any good papers / ideas to read in this direction would be helpful.   
* **Model**: A linear model predicting difficulty $$\\beta\_i$$ from problem input features $$x\_i$$. 

We train and use this difficulty predictor model in the following way: 

* **Training:** Train IRT on just the train tasks. Train the linear model to minimize the (regularized) L2 loss of the linear model predicting the learned IRT difficulties from the task features (ridge regression) of the training set.   
* **Inference:** LLM as judge tells us $$x\_i$$ on a new, unseen question, linear model predicts difficulty $$\\beta\_i$$ in the scale of our train set IRT model (**without having to relearn the IRT parameters\!**), IRT (using the model abilities that we learned during training time) predicts the probability that that agent gets that problem correct.   
  * Note that we never train an IRT model on the entire dataset, even during evaluation. The only time we do is for the comparison to the Oracle   
* **Evaluation**: ROC-AUC on held out tasks.   
  * ![][image1]  
  * ![][image2]  
  * current status of our approaches

### 2.3 Goal 2: The Failure-Informed Posterior (Weak to Strong generalization)

This entire section besides the main objective is up in the air and unclear if it will work.  I have not been able to get nontrivial spearman rho performance  

**Objective:** Refine the difficulty estimate of really hard problems using failure trajectory analysis from agents too weak to solve the problems.

The key idea is that we restrict ourselves to only using models from before August 7, 2025 (somewhat arbitrary cutoff date, it’s when gpt-5-mini was released) during the training process, so that we have “oracle data” from the “future” for validation and actually know the true difficulties of these “too-hard” problems. But anything else from the present day is fair game for use. 

The model we’ll use is quite different from goal 1\. Most notably, our IRT model changes to include a $$\\psi\_{ij}$$ term which represents the subjective relative performance of the agent $$j$$ on that task $$i$$, as in [SAD-IRT](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/artificial-intelligence/articles/10.3389/frai.2023.1324279/full): 

$$P(y\_{ij} \= 1 \\mid \\theta\_j, \\alpha\_i, \\beta\_i, \\psi\_{ij}) \= \\sigma(\\alpha\_i(\\theta\_j \- (\\beta\_i \+ \\psi\_{ij})))$$

* Think of $$\\psi\_{ij}$$ as a correction term for the agent’s actual performance (i.e. is it underperforming, overperforming). Somewhat importantly, $$\\psi\_{ij}$$ must have mean $$0$$ across $$i, j$$, otherwise $$\\beta\_i$$ and the $$\\psi\_{ij}$$’s would not be identifiable up to constant shift.   
  * In practice, these $$\\psi\_{ij}$$ terms will come from a Qwen3-0.6B \+ a linear head, fitted with LoRA adapters, with input being the summarized trajectories. The linear head and LoRA adapters will be trained during training time, along with the $$\\theta, \\beta$$ parameters.  I’m not sure whether or not to include the true response in the summarized trajectory, as it should be possible to overfit since we know what the true response is based on that, but probably the embeddings are too dumb to capture this  
* This incorporates the trajectory information for a task $$i$$ without us having to do some kind of contrived averaging of features over all trajectories for the task. We can just read off the task difficulty $$\\beta\_i$$ 

The constraints of the setting and the affordances we’re given ourselves work together in a really weird way to constrain us. This is best illustrated in the validation 

* **Training**: We train our IRT variant only using response performance of models from before August 7, 2025, to maximize log likelihood of the observed responses.   
* **Validation**: At the end of the day, we care about the difficulty of the “too difficult” problems, and our gold standard is our standard IRT difficulty estimates of these problems using all models (including models after August 7).   
  * There’s no ROC-AUC calculation that we can do here that really makes sense. We can’t calculate ROC-AUC on the held out recent models on these problems using the true ability scores of the recent models and our predicted IRT difficulty scores for these problems, because our predicted IRT difficulty scores and the true IRT difficulty scores are on different scales, due to being trained on different models.   
  * We’ll instead settle for now on spearman rho between the difficulty that we predict for those problems, and the true IRT difficulty of those problems.   
  * For now, “too difficult” problems will be problems on which the pre August 7 models get \< 10% performance on, while post August 7 models get ≥ 10%.   
    * concern: only have 13 of these problems with trajectories 

### 2.4: Anomaly Detection in Benchmarks 

Somewhat orthogonal to everything else

Can we use trajectories where dumb (low IRT ability) agents succeed on hard (high IRT difficulty) problems to debug dataset problems? 

![][image3]  
In practice, out of these tasks, all but 2 seem bad in one of three ways: 

1. The task was too vague and the tests tested something that was not specified in the task, so a lot of strong agents got it wrong, artificially increasing the difficulty. But the gold patch was still very short, and agentless\_MCTS\_Refine-7B found the exact gold patch  
2. The tests weren’t strong enough, and they allowed very shallow patches to pass, which let some weak models solve the problem, while stronger models struggled with the whole complex fix  
3. The problem was genuinely pretty hard, but there was a clever short / naive approach that turns out to actually work 

Having the trajectories helps, as you can see exactly what the dumb model is doing that the smart model isn’t doing. I don’t know how to present this finding or what experiments to run from here. 

---

## **3 Experimental Plan**

### 3.1 Experiment A: Prior Validation (IRT AUC)

Run the setup in Section 2.2. 

* **Evaluate** AUC on a held-out test set of tasks to see if we can predict agents' pass@k success probability zero-shot.   
* Use as **baseline** just predicting that agent’s average performance, and compare LLM as judge and embeddings, with and without solution access. 

### 3.2 Experiment B: Posterior Validation (IRT Difficulty Prediction for Harder Tasks) 

Run the setup in section 2.3  
Our baseline is just standard IRT trained on the pre-August 7 models (which should have little to no information on the “too hard” problems, as fewer than 10% of attempts on them are successes)   
![][image4]

Extra plots / subexperiments: 

* retroactively forecast model arrival dates for models from the last 6 months  
* Can we do any interpretation of the $$\\psi\_{ij}$$ variable? 

### 3.3 Experiment C: Compute Costs

Running LLM as judge / embeddings on just the problem is much faster and hopefully cheaper than actually running an agent on the task multiple times.

### 3.4 Experiment D: Time Horizon Prediction — COMPLETED {#3.4-experiment-d:-time-horizon-prediction-—-completed}

![][image5]

This linear trend lets us convert from IRT difficulty score to when in real life a frontier model will first be able to achieve 50% pass probability on problems of that IRT difficulty score. Since our other methods let us estimate the IRT difficulty score, this allows us to ground back to a real life date. 

note: we’d have to re-fit this linear trend if we decide to retrain the IRT parameters using a different method, like I propose in one of the experiments

## **4 Expectations and timeline** 

We hope to submit one combined paper for me and Daria to COLM, but get most of the experiments done by the end of January.
